ameliyatsiz-varis-tedavisi.title: Non-surgical varicose vein treatment
ameliyatsiz-varis-tedavisi.excerpt: Foam, laser and radiofrequency methods for comfortable, incision-free treatment. Same-day discharge.
body: Varicose veins develop when superficial veins dilate and valves fail, often causing pain, heaviness and cosmetic concerns. Non-surgical treatment closes or shrinks these veins using laser, radiofrequency or foam sclerotherapy without open surgery.

Colour Doppler ultrasound maps the veins and guides the choice of technique. Large truncal veins are often treated with endovenous laser or radiofrequency; smaller veins and spider veins with foam sclerotherapy.
Procedures are performed under local anaesthesia or light sedation with ultrasound guidance. Most patients go home the same day. Compression stockings and walking are recommended during recovery.
Benefits include minimal scarring, faster return to daily activities and good cosmetic outcomes. Follow-up ultrasound confirms closure.

faq.a: Local anaesthesia keeps discomfort minimal. Mild soreness or bruising may occur and usually settles with simple pain relief.
Many patients are discharged the same day and return to routine activities within a few days, following your doctor’s advice.
Treated veins typically remain closed. New veins can develop over time; additional sessions can be planned if needed.

tiroid-nodul-tedavisi.title: Non-surgical thyroid nodule treatment
tiroid-nodul-tedavisi.excerpt: Ultrasound-guided microwave ablation for targeted treatment, usually same-day discharge.
body: Thyroid nodules are lumps within the thyroid. Benign nodules that cause pressure, cosmetic issues or growth can be treated with thermal ablation (microwave or laser) without removing the gland.
Fine-needle aspiration biopsy is used to confirm benign disease before ablation. The procedure is planned according to nodule size, number and symptoms.
Under local anaesthesia a thin needle is advanced into the nodule under ultrasound; thermal energy destroys targeted tissue while preserving healthy thyroid.
Nodules shrink gradually over months. Thyroid function is usually preserved. Follow-up ultrasound monitors the response.

faq.a: Local anaesthesia makes the procedure tolerable; mild neck discomfort may occur for a few days.
Most nodules shrink significantly; the goal is symptom relief and halting growth.

girisimsel-radyoloji.title: Interventional radiology procedures
girisimsel-radyoloji.excerpt: Image-guided minimally invasive diagnosis and treatment without open surgery.
body: Interventional radiology uses ultrasound, CT, fluoroscopy or MRI to guide needles and catheters for biopsy, drainage, vascular access and tumour ablation.
Common procedures include image-guided biopsy, abscess drainage, port placement, varicose vein treatment and thyroid ablation.
Most interventions use local anaesthesia or sedation; many patients are discharged the same day after a short observation period.
Benefits include smaller access sites, less pain and quicker recovery compared with traditional open surgery when clinically appropriate.

faq.a: Image guidance targets the lesion precisely; sterile technique and protocols keep complication rates low.
Many procedures are done with local anaesthesia; sedation or general anaesthesia is used when needed.

biyopsi.title: Biopsy procedures
biyopsi.excerpt: Breast, thyroid, liver and lung biopsy under ultrasound or CT guidance with minimal discomfort.
body: Biopsy obtains tissue for pathology when imaging shows a suspicious lesion. Image guidance improves accuracy and safety.
Fine-needle aspiration collects cells; core biopsy obtains small tissue cores for detailed analysis. The approach depends on lesion location and clinical question.
The procedure usually takes 15–30 minutes with local anaesthesia. Anticoagulants may need temporary adjustment under medical supervision.
Mild bruising or soreness is common. Results typically return within several working days and guide further management.

faq.a: Tell your doctor about all medications; some may need to be paused according to a personalised plan.
Image-guided core and fine-needle techniques have an extremely low risk of seeding and are internationally accepted as safe.

lazerle-varis-tedavisi.title: Laser varicose vein treatment
lazerle-varis-tedavisi.excerpt: Endovenous laser ablation from inside the vein; short recovery and durable closure.
body: Endovenous laser ablation (EVLA) treats incompetent superficial veins by heating the wall from inside using laser energy delivered through a thin fibre.
Ultrasound maps the target vein and guides catheter placement. Tumescent fluid around the vein improves comfort and protects surrounding tissues.
The procedure commonly takes 30–60 minutes; patients usually walk shortly afterwards and go home the same day.
Compression stockings and regular walking support healing. Follow-up ultrasound confirms successful closure.

faq.a: Typically 30–60 minutes depending on vein length and anatomy.
Compression is usually advised for several weeks to optimise outcomes.

radyofrekansla-varis-tedavisi.title: Radiofrequency varicose vein treatment
radyofrekansla-varis-tedavisi.excerpt: Radiofrequency energy closes incompetent veins under local anaesthesia with same-day mobilisation.
body: Radiofrequency ablation (RFA) heats the vein wall using controlled RF energy delivered through an intravenous catheter, leading to contraction and closure.
Like laser, RFA is used for truncal incompetence after ultrasound mapping. Choice between laser and RF depends on anatomy and physician preference.
The session typically lasts 30–60 minutes with local anaesthesia or sedation. Compression and walking are encouraged afterwards.
Outcomes are comparable to laser for many patients, with excellent long-term closure rates when indicated.

faq.a: Both aim to close the vein thermally; your specialist will recommend the best option for your anatomy.

kopuk-skleroterapi.title: Foam sclerotherapy
kopuk-skleroterapi.excerpt: Foam injection for reticular and spider veins; incision-free, often walk-in sessions.
body: Foam sclerotherapy mixes sclerosant with gas or air to create microfoam that contacts the vein wall effectively, even for slightly larger superficial veins.
It is commonly used for spider veins, reticular veins and as an adjunct after endovenous treatment of truncal veins.
Sessions are short; several treatments spaced weeks apart may be needed. Compression and sun protection help skin healing.
Side effects are usually mild, such as temporary pigmentation or bruising that fades over time.

faq.a: Often 1–4 sessions depending on vein density and size, spaced a few weeks apart.

4d-gebe-ultrasonu.title: 4D pregnancy ultrasound
4d-gebe-ultrasonu.excerpt: Real-time 3D imaging of fetal face and movements for memorable bonding moments.
body: 4D ultrasound adds the time dimension to 3D imaging so parents can see fetal movements and facial expressions in near real time.
The best imaging window is often between 24 and 32 weeks when amniotic fluid and fetal position are favourable.
Ultrasound does not use ionising radiation and is considered safe for diagnostic doses. Routine 2D scans remain the standard for medical assessment.
4D is primarily an bonding experience; structural screening is still documented with 2D imaging as indicated.

faq.a: Yes—diagnostic ultrasound uses sound waves, not X-rays, at doses considered safe in guidelines.

port-takilmasi.title: Port-a-cath placement
port-takilmasi.excerpt: Subcutaneous port for chemotherapy or long-term venous access—comfortable and reliable.
body: A totally implantable venous access port consists of a reservoir under the skin connected to a catheter in a central vein, reducing repeated needle sticks.
Ports are commonly placed for chemotherapy, long-term antibiotics or parenteral nutrition using imaging guidance.
The procedure is usually performed under sedation or general anaesthesia with fluoroscopy or ultrasound to confirm catheter tip position.
After healing, the port is accessed with a Huber needle using sterile technique; periodic flushing helps maintain patency.

faq.q: How long can a port stay in place?
faq.a: With proper care, ports may remain for months to years depending on treatment needs.

kist-abse-drenaji.title: Cyst and abscess drainage
kist-abse-drenaji.excerpt: Ultrasound- or CT-guided drainage for rapid relief of fluid collections.
body: Image-guided drainage uses a needle or catheter to evacuate cyst fluid or abscess pus, avoiding open surgery in selected cases.
Abscess samples may be sent for culture to guide antibiotics. Some cysts can be sclerosed to reduce recurrence.
Local anaesthesia is typical; many patients go home the same day or after short observation.
Follow-up imaging confirms resolution and guides further steps if needed.

faq.a: Proper drainage and antibiotics reduce recurrence; some cysts may need additional treatment.

bilgisayarli-tomografi.title: Computed tomography (CT)
bilgisayarli-tomografi.excerpt: Cross-sectional X-ray imaging for detailed evaluation of organs, vessels and bone.
body: CT provides rapid, high-resolution images of the head, chest, abdomen, pelvis and extremities for trauma, infection, tumour work-up and emergency conditions.
Intravenous contrast can highlight vessels and lesions; kidney function and allergy history are reviewed beforehand.
Examination times are usually short. Radiation dose is kept as low as reasonably achievable using modern protocols.
Pregnancy requires special consideration; urgent decisions are made together with your clinician.

faq.q: How long does a CT scan take?
faq.a: Often 5–20 minutes depending on the region and whether contrast is used.

mr-goruntuleme.title: MRI
mr-goruntuleme.excerpt: Radiation-free detailed imaging of soft tissues, brain, spine and joints.
body: MRI uses magnetic fields and radio waves to create detailed images without ionising radiation, valuable for brain, spine, musculoskeletal and many body applications.
Some studies use gadolinium-based contrast after renal function assessment. The scanner is noisy; ear protection is provided.
Metal implants, pacemakers and foreign bodies must be disclosed before scanning—some devices are MRI-conditional.
Claustrophobia can be managed with communication strategies or mild sedation when appropriate.

faq.a: No—MRI does not use X-rays.

faq.q: Can I have MRI with a pacemaker?
faq.a: Only MRI-conditional systems with documented settings are safe; always inform staff before scanning.

xray-goruntuleme.title: X-ray imaging
xray-goruntuleme.excerpt: Fast conventional radiography for bones, chest and abdomen.
body: X-ray remains a quick first-line tool for fractures, chest pathology and selected abdominal conditions.
Metal objects are removed when possible. Pregnancy is discussed to shield or defer non-urgent studies.
Digital systems allow efficient storage and dose optimisation for children and adults.
Results are reported by radiologists and correlated with your referring physician’s question.

faq.q: How long does an X-ray take?
faq.a: The exposure is seconds; including positioning, the visit usually takes only a few minutes.

mamografi.title: Mammography and breast imaging
mamografi.excerpt: Breast cancer screening and diagnosis with low-dose X-ray mammography.
body: Mammography compresses the breast briefly to obtain high-quality images and is central to early breast cancer detection.
Screening intervals depend on age and risk factors; diagnostic mammography evaluates symptoms or abnormal findings.
Tomosynthesis (3D mammography) can improve conspicuity of lesions in dense tissue. Ultrasound or MRI may supplement selected cases.
Results use standardised reporting (e.g. BI-RADS) to guide follow-up or biopsy when needed.

faq.q: How often should screening be done?
faq.a: Many programmes suggest yearly screening from around age 40; personalised schedules apply for higher risk.
Compression is tight but brief; scheduling after your period may reduce sensitivity.
